sentiment.label: risk_off
sentiment.reason_it: Il sentiment di mercato è risk-off a causa dell'aumento del VIX del 13.16% a 31.05, indicando un mercato difensivo. Il DXY è salito dello 0.25% a 100.15, mentre il prezzo del petrolio è aumentato del 4.22% a $112.57. Il TLT è sceso dello 0.55% a 85.64, riflettendo una riduzione dell'appetito per i risk assets.
sentiment.reason_en: Market sentiment is risk-off due to the 13.16% increase in VIX to 31.05, indicating a defensive market. The DXY rose 0.25% to 100.15, while oil prices increased by 4.22% to $112.57. TLT fell 0.55% to 85.64, reflecting reduced appetite for risk assets.

market_impact_summary.it: Il mercato azionario è chiuso per il fine settimana, ma i dati di mercato mostrano un sentiment risk-off. Il VIX è aumentato significativamente, raggiungendo 31.05. Il prezzo del petrolio è salito a $112.57, mentre il DXY si trova a 100.15. Il TLT è sceso a 85.64. Le crypto sono in calo, con Bitcoin a $66,305 e Ethereum a $1,993.
market_impact_summary.en: The stock market is closed for the weekend, but market data shows a risk-off sentiment. VIX significantly increased to 31.05. Oil prices rose to $112.57, while DXY stands at 100.15. TLT fell to 85.64. Cryptos are down, with Bitcoin at $66,305 and Ethereum at $1,993.

audio_script_it:
Mentre le borse mondiali osservano la consueta pausa festiva del fine settimana, i riflettori restano accesi sul comparto digitale, che opera 24 ore su 24. La chiusura dei mercati asiatici ci consegna un Nikkei in calo dello 0.43% a 53,373 punti e uno Shanghai in crescita dello 0.63% a 3913.72 punti. Questi movimenti anticipano una possibile apertura cauta delle borse europee e americane, qualora fossero aperte.

La seduta asiatica si è conclusa con risultati contrastanti. Il Nikkei ha registrato un calo dello 0.43%, mentre lo Shanghai ha mostrato una crescita dello 0.63%. L'Hang Seng di Hong Kong ha chiuso in aumento dello 0.38%. Questi dati suggeriscono una prudenza generalizzata in Asia, che potrebbe influenzare le aperture dei mercati occidentali, se aperti.

Il sentiment attuale è decisamente risk-off, come evidenziato dai mercati occidentali nella chiusura precedente. L'S&P 500 ha perso l'1.67% a 6368.85 punti, mentre il VIX è salito del 13.16% a 31.05, riflettendo un aumento dell'avversione al rischio. Il Dollar Index (DXY) ha guadagnato lo 0.25% a 100.15 punti, e il rendimento del decennale USA è salito a 4.44%. L'oro ha guadagnato il 2.66% a $4492.00/oz, mentre il prezzo del petrolio Brent è salito del 4.22% a $112.57.

Sul fronte geopolitico, la situazione rimane tesa. La Nato ha annunciato che gli alleati effettueranno esercitazioni militari con l'Ucraina, mentre gli Stati Uniti e altri paesi occidentali continuano a monitorare la situazione in Iran. Queste dinamiche potrebbero influenzare i mercati nella prossima settimana.

In ambito macroeconomico, gli ultimi dati disponibili non sono recenti, ma segnalano una tendenza verso una stabilizzazione dell'inflazione e un rallentamento dell'economia. La BCE ha recentemente mantenuto invariato il tasso di interesse al 2.00%. La prossima settimana sarà importante per l'uscita di nuovi dati, come il PIL del primo trimestre degli USA e l'indice dei prezzi al consumo dell'Eurozona.

Passando al comparto digitale, oggi i mercati crypto operano in territorio negativo. Il Bitcoin ha perso il 3.61% a $66,305, mentre Ethereum e Solana hanno registrato cali del 3.18% e del 3.86% rispettivamente. L'indice Fear & Greed è sceso a 12, in territorio di Extreme Fear. Gli afflussi netti nei fondi ETF Bitcoin sono risultati negativi per $171.3 milioni.

Per la prossima settimana, l'attenzione sarà focalizzata sull'evoluzione della situazione geopolitica e sugli sviluppi macroeconomici. I mercati tradizionali riprenderanno la loro attività lunedì, mentre il comparto crypto continuerà a operare 24/7. Sarà importante monitorare i nuovi dati economici e le mosse delle banche centrali per comprendere meglio la direzione dei mercati.

Passiamo ora al comparto degli asset digitali, con un'analisi approfondita delle criptovalute.

Iniziamo con il Bitcoin, che ha registrato un calo del 3.27% portandosi a $66,315. Gli ETF Bitcoin hanno visto un deflusso di $-171.3M, il più grande outflow delle ultime tre settimane, in concomitanza con l'aumento delle tensioni geopolitiche. Questo potrebbe indicare una maggiore avversione al rischio da parte degli investitori istituzionali.

Passando alle altcoin, Ethereum è sceso del 3.18% a $1,993, mentre Solana ha perso il 3.86% portandosi a $82.65. Binance Coin (BNB) ha registrato un calo del 2.39% a $611.91. Questi movimenti sono in linea con il trend generale di mercato, che sembra essere influenzato dalle preoccupazioni sull'inflazione e sulla stabilità geopolitica.

L'Indice di Paura ed Avidità si trova a 12, in territorio di Estrema Paura. Questo livello suggerisce che gli investitori sono estremamente cauti e possibilmente sottovalutano il mercato. Storicamente, livelli di Estrema Paura sono stati associati a opportunità di acquisto a lungo termine, ma è essenziale considerare anche altri fattori macroeconomici e tecnici.

In sintesi, il mercato delle criptovalute sembra essere in una fase di consolidamento, con gli investitori che stanno reagendo alle notizie macroeconomiche e geopolitiche. È importante tenere d'occhio gli sviluppi futuri, come la prossima uscita del PIL QoQ USA e le decisioni della BCE, per capire meglio come potrebbero evolversi i mercati.

Grazie per l'attenzione e a domani
audio_script_en:
Welcome to your daily morning market briefing. Good morning and welcome to today's financial update.

Global sentiment is risk-off today, with investors digesting a mix of economic data and geopolitical tensions. Key data points include a higher-than-expected VIX index at 31.05, up 13.16%, and a significant increase in gold prices to $4492.00/oz, up 2.66%.

Traditional markets are closed for the weekend, but looking at the last available data: equities were under pressure with the S&P 500 at 6368.85, down 1.67%, and the STOXX 600 at 57.05, down 0.90%. Bond yields were slightly higher, with the US 10Y Yield at 4.44%, up 0.54%, and the BTP 10Y at 4.05%, up 0.80%. Currencies saw the Dollar Index (DXY) strengthen to 100.15, up 0.25%, while the EUR/USD pair weakened to 1.15, down 0.23%. Commodities were mixed, with Brent crude up 4.22% to $112.57 and gold prices surging.

Geopolitical tensions continue to influence market sentiment, with concerns over a potential escalation in the Middle East and the ongoing situation in Ukraine. These developments are keeping investors cautious and supportive of safe-haven assets. The macroeconomic landscape is also being closely watched, with global M2 liquidity continuing to expand, up 4.9% YoY to $22.7T.

While traditional markets observe their holiday break, the spotlight remains on digital assets, which trade around the clock. Let's take a look at the current state of the crypto market.

Moving on to the digital assets space...

The Bitcoin price has dipped under $66,000, currently trading at $66,315, down 3.27% over the past 24 hours. From a technical standpoint, the recent BTC ETF daily net outflow of $171.3 million has likely contributed to the downward pressure. On-chain data suggests that whales and sharks have been accumulating, buying 61,000 BTC in a month, possibly anticipating future gains.

Let's pivot to the cryptocurrency markets and take a look at some of the major altcoins. Ethereum is trading at $1,993, down 3.18%, while Solana is at $82.65, down 3.86%. Binance Coin is also in the red, trading at $611.91, a 2.39% decrease. These altcoins have been closely correlated with Bitcoin's price movements, and their declines mirror the overall market sentiment.

Sentiment in the market remains bearish, with the Fear & Greed Index reading 12, indicating extreme fear. This index takes into account various factors such as price momentum, market volatility, and social media sentiment. Historically, extreme fear has been a buying opportunity for contrarian investors. However, it's essential to consider the broader market context, including macroeconomic factors and potential regulatory developments, such as the recent crypto tax proposal from US lawmakers.

Thanks for tuning in. We'll be back tomorrow with another update. Until then, stay informed and stay ahead. A wrap from Prais Alért. Al prossimo aggiornamento.

articles.title: The realities of economic security
articles.snippet: The Peterson Institute for International Economics presents a high-level analytical discussion of the realities of economic security, examining real-world trade-offs and vulnerabilities in today's geopolitically fractured world. Anjali Bhatt, host of PIIE Insider Live, moderates a candid discussion with PIIE experts: Warwick McKibbin, who presents global macroeconomic scenarios of the impact of the Middle East conflict; Cullen Hendrix, who discusses vulnerabilities of the global economy to the disrupted supply of commodities including food, fuel, and industrial inputs; and Jacob Funk…

articles.title: The Pentagon Wants Dual-Use Innovation. Patent Law Might Punish It.
articles.snippet: In 1918, the U.S. Supreme Court held that government contractors could be sued for patent infringement even if their products were manufactured specifically for the U.S. government. Franklin D. Roosevelt, then acting secretary of the Navy, wrote an urgent letter to Congress warning that contractors were hesitant to build equipment for the Navy that could expose them to expensive litigation. Congress responded swiftly, amending federal law to shield contractors from patent suits when they were manufacturing products for the government. That statutory immunity, now codified as 28 U.S.C. § 1498, has protected defense manufacturers for over a century. But a The post The Pentagon Wants Dual-Use Innovation. Patent Law Might Punish It. appeared first on War on the Rocks .

articles.title: Why the Iran Conflict Is Good News for Chinese Automakers
articles.snippet: A sharp rise in oil prices tied to the US-Israel confrontation with Iran is likely to speed up the global transition to electric vehicles, strengthening a shift that has already helped China overtake Japan as the world’s top car seller, according to South China Morning Post. Crude prices have surged past $100 a barrel amid fears of disruption to energy supplies, particularly through the Strait of Hormuz. US President Donald Trump escalated tensions by warning he would “obliterate” Iran’s power plants if shipping through…

articles.summary_en: The Iran conflict could speed up the transition to electric vehicles, benefiting Chinese automakers.

articles.title: Russia Warns of Force Majeure on Oil Cargoes After Port Disruptions
articles.snippet: Russia’s oil exporters are warning buyers that cargoes from its key Baltic ports may not be delivered at all. Russian producers are now saying they may declare force majeure on cargoes from key Baltic Sea ports after a sustained wave of Ukrainian drone strikes knocked out critical infrastructure this week. The epicenter is Ust-Luga, one of Russia’s most important export terminals, where oil loadings have been halted since Wednesday following repeated strikes from Ukraine and a fire that, as of Friday, was still burning. Industry sources…

articles.title: $100 Oil Is Solving Russia's Budget Problem
articles.snippet: Russia is getting an unexpected windfall from the war in the Middle East. The Kremlin’s oil revenues this month hit a four-year high as oil prices jumped to $100 per barrel amid the Iran war and the de facto closed Strait of Hormuz. Moscow expects so much additional revenues from the oil price spike that authorities are unlikely to downgrade Russia’s economic prospects, hold off on planned budget cuts, and even boost military spending on the war in Ukraine, Bloomberg reports, citing sources with knowledge of the matter. A month ago,…

articles.title: Gulf States Weigh a Difficult Choice as Iran War Escalates
articles.snippet: Caught in the crossfire of the United States and Israel's war with Iran, America's allies in the Persian Gulf face a stark choice: maintain their defensive posture or join the fight against the Islamic Republic. Iran has fired thousands of drones and missiles at US military and diplomatic facilities and damaged key energy infrastructure in Saudi Arabia, the United Arab Emirates (UAE), Qatar, Bahrain, and Kuwait since the war began on February 28. By choking off the Strait of Hormuz, a major artery for global oil and gas supplies, Tehran has also…

articles.title: A Key Russia-Iran Supply Line Remains Open Despite the War
articles.snippet: All the bombs dropped by the United States and Israel over the past four weeks on Iran have not disrupted a key trade route with Russia that both Tehran and Moscow rely on to help keep their respective war efforts going. And Azerbaijan, despite its strong ties to both the United States and Israel, has played a crucial role in keeping the route open. Russian Deputy Prime Minister Alexey Overchuk, in comments published by the Interfax news agency on March 25, said the “western branch” of the North-South trade corridor continued to operate…
